summary: Validated legacy LargeSystemCache reset. Win25H2Clean reversible probe confirmed LargeSystemCache stayed at 0 under Memory Management and restored the baseline.

setting.name: Large System Cache
setting.casual_explanation: Windows historically had a switch between favoring normal applications and favoring a larger system cache. Most normal users should leave this alone on modern systems.
setting.why_people_change_it: People only change it when they are undoing very old file-server or memory-cache tuning and want to restore an application-focused client baseline.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: The app only writes the legacy client value 0. Microsoft still marks the property obsolete, so this should be read as a reset-to-client-baseline action, not as a modern performance recommendation.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft's Win32_OperatingSystem class still describes LargeSystemCache with 0 meaning 'Optimize for Applications' and 1 meaning 'Optimize for System Performance', but the same property is marked obsolete and not supported. The current Memory Limits page only mentions that old Windows XP and Windows Server 2003 limits changed when the LargeSystemCache registry key was set. That means this is not a modern recommended tuning surface, but the current app behavior is still exact and narrow: it writes the legacy client reset value LargeSystemCache = 0, and the Win25H2Clean reversible probe showed the value was already 0 and restored cleanly.

allowed_values.label: Optimize for applications
allowed_values.meaning: The historical semantics exposed by Win32_OperatingSystem say memory is optimized for applications.

allowed_values.label: Optimize for system performance
allowed_values.meaning: The historical semantics exposed by Win32_OperatingSystem say memory is optimized for system performance.

app_current_implementation.notes: Guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the current app write is idempotent at the baseline LargeSystemCache=0 value.

windows_defaults.label: Conservative publication baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Modern systems where this dataset does not yet publish LargeSystemCache as a supported tuning surface

states.rationale: Keep the legacy client value 0 used by the current app and confirmed by the VM probe.

windows_defaults.notes: This remains a legacy surface, but the current app action is an exact reset to the client baseline.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research
intended_for: Systems undoing an old server-style cache tweak

states.rationale: Do not treat this as a modern tuning control; only use it as a legacy reset reference.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the setting without overstating confidence.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the app to actively reset the legacy client value.
tradeoffs: The app behavior is documented honestly.
The setting remains obsolete and stays out of one-click apply.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile
intended_for: Systems undoing an old server-style cache tweak

states.rationale: Observed app write representing the client/application-focused mode.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the app to restore the legacy client/applications value 0.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want a modern, fully supported Microsoft registry control.
tradeoffs: The control remains marked obsolete and unsupported by Microsoft.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Win32_OperatingSystem LargeSystemCache
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the historical 0 and 1 semantics for LargeSystemCache but marks the property obsolete and not supported.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Memory Limits for Windows Releases
evidence.summary: Microsoft notes that older Windows XP and Windows Server 2003 system-cache limits changed when the LargeSystemCache registry key was set, confirming the key's historical existence but not publishing a modern tuning recommendation.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes LargeSystemCache = 0 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft still publishes the legacy 0/1 semantics, the current app writes only the client reset value 0, and the Win25H2Clean probe confirmed that write is idempotent and reversible. The property stays obsolete, but the current app action is narrow enough to expose as an exact reset-to-client-baseline mapping.

validation_proof.notes: Guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean; see the batch probe output in evidence/files/vm-tooling-staging/vm-batch-probe-20260320.json..md
